content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
July 17,
.1992
James R.
Sutton
Nielsen,
Merksamer,
Hodgson,
Parrinello and Mueller
591 Redwood Highway,
#4000
Mill Valley,
CA
94941
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—92—351
Dear Mr.
Sutton:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
You have requested informal assistance.
2
Please note that nothing
in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which
may have already taken place.
In addition, this letter is based
on the facts presented to us.
QUESTION
Does the “protocol” exception apply to gifts from business
entities to an official, to be used to host a reception for
foreign dignitaries and business leaders?
CONCLUSION
The “protocol” exception set forth in the White Advice
Letter,
No.
1-92—218,
does not apply to gifts from business
entities to an official to be used to host a reception for foreign
dignitaries and business leaders.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 I
P.O. Box 807 I
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-92-351
Page
2
FACTS
You represent a company which has been asked by a public
official to donate money for a reception that the official would
like to host for foreign dignitaries and business leaders.
The
official will develop the invitation list.
You have asked whether
the official has received a gift of the full value of the event.
ANALYSI S
As you note in your advice request,
where gifts are given to
an official with the understanding that the official will
distribute the gifts to others at the official’s discretion, the
gifts are deemed to be gifts to the public official.
3
(White
Advice Letter,
No.
1-92-218.)
However,
in the White Advice Letter
we identified an exception to this rule.
The White Letter
considered whether gift tickets from the Walt Disney Company to
persons at the recommendation or suggestion of public officials,
including Anaheim City Councilmembers and the city manager would
be gifts to the requesting official who never actually came into
possession of the tickets.
In that letter,
we described the “protocol” exception.
We
stated that where free tickets to Disneyland were provided to
other public officials or visiting dignitaries by Disneyland at
the recommendation or request of members of the Anaheim City
Council or Anaheim City Manager acting in their official capacity,
the requesting official would not have received a gift.
However, the exception was not applicable to tickets in the
possession of the official and redistributed by the official.
We
cautioned:
In order to avoid the problem of being a
recipient of a gift in the circumstances described
here,
we recommend that the city official refer the
third party to the tourist attraction and that the
third party make his or her own arrangements for
complimentary admission tickets with the attraction
directly.
Provided that the official or his or her
agent does not make a suggestion,
recommendation or
request to the tourist attraction that it pvides
complimentary tickets for a third party (subject to
the “protocol exception” noted above),
does not
deliver the tickets to the third party,
and does
not get involved in the ticket arrangements in any
way other than the referral, the city official
would not be considered the recipient of those free
tickets.
You have not provided sufficient facts to determine if in fact
the payment would be a gift, rather than a contribution to the
official.


Our File No.
1-92-351
Page
3
Factually, your situation is different from that discussed in
the White letter.
For example,
under the facts in the White
letter the Walt Disney Company itself determines whether it will
honor, modify, or reject the recommendation of a public official
or other requestor for complimentary tickets for third parties.
If Disney elected to honor an official’s recommendation and
provide free admission tickets to the recommended group or
individual,
Disneyland left the tickets at its “Will Call” window
in the name of that group or individual.
The recommending official did not determine whether the free
tickets would be issued to the third parties; the recommending
official did not handle the tickets; and, the recommending
official could not themselves pick up the tickets at the “Will
Call” window since the tickets were held in the name of the third
party recipient.
None of these facts appear to apply to your
situation.
4
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-59Ol.
Since ely,
cott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
JWW: SH: aa
Please note, however, where funds are donated to a public
agency for such an event,
the official’s participation would most
likely not result in his or her receipt of a gift.
(See e.g.,
In
re Stone
(1977)
3
FPPC Ops.
52.)
However,
you have not presented
facts to indicate that this might be an agency event.
Please
contact us for further advice if this is the case.
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 1O per page.
